# Government backs down on charging older Australians $50/hour for showers

By political reporter [Evelyn Manfield](https://www.abc.net.au/news/evelyn-manfield/11530668) and national health reporter [Stephanie Dalzell](https://www.abc.net.au/news/stephanie-dalzell/3318210)

- [Topic:Aged Care](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/aged-care)

9h ago9 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 12:38pm

![A greying man in a suit.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4954da6789cd0640c94cba08f2ff7a8f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3510&cropW=6240&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

Mark Butler is set to announce the reversal at the National Press Club on Wednesday.(ABC News: Matt Roberts)

## In short:

The federal government will dump a controversial decision that charged at-home aged care recipients for showers, dressing and continence care.

It follows months of sustained criticism, including warnings that some older people were forgoing other care to afford showers.

## What's next?

The government has revealed fully covering the extra care will cost $1 billion over the next four years.

The federal government has moved to dump a controversial decision that led to some older Australians paying $50 an hour for basic care like showering, just six months after the sweeping changes took effect.

A long-awaited overhaul to aged care included older people on a Support at Home package co-paying for services, in a government effort to rein in spending in the sector.

Under the scheme, [which began in November](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-09-22/older-australians-to-pay-for-showers-under-home-care-changes/105796118), pensioners, part-pensioners and self-funded retirees paid out-of-pocket between 5 and 50 per cent of the service provider's fee for support, including showers, which worked out to be about $50 an hour for some.

The financial hit meant some Support at Home recipients were forced to shower less or forgo other care to pay for it.

Loading...

Following sustained criticism from advocates and older Australians who described the scheme as an "obscenity", the federal government has backed down, with Aged Care Minister Sam Rae conceding showering is an essential service.

"Showering, dressing, continence care — these aren't optional extras. They're the basics of aging with dignity, and no older Australian should miss out because of cost," he said.

> "Older Australians, their families and providers told us these services needed to be protected. We've listened, and we're acting."

The change will take effect from October, but co-payments for non-clinical care will continue.

Mr Rae has confirmed the policy shift would cost the budget $1 billion over the next four years.

"We've gone through a careful policy consideration process. Of course, this system has only been in place since November," he said.

"Now is the time to start making the adjustments that get better outcomes for older people."

## Butler to unveil backdown in major speech

Health Minister Mark Butler is set to officially unveil the reversal during a speech at the National Press Club in Canberra on Wednesday, where he will reveal how the government plans to stem the growth of the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS), with some of the money saved to be diverted to aged care.

Council on the Ageing (COTA) acting chief executive Corey Irlam said the announcement was welcome but overdue.

"Basic care like showering and continence should never have been treated like optional extras," he said.

"This is about dignity — no-one should have to choose between affordability and basic personal care."

![A man wearing a suit stands at the dispatch box in the House of Representatives.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f1c645896afcaad15e470221aabf9c4f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Sam Rae says basic care should never have been treated as an optional extra.(ABC News: Matt Roberts)

Older Persons Advocacy Network's (OPAN) Samantha Edmond said her organisation received reports of people paying "from $50 upwards" for showering, which was "prohibitive" and "unreasonable".

She said OPAN was "certainly surprised to see charges put on showering" in the first place.

Chief executive of Ageing Australia, Tom Symondson, who represents providers, also backed the move as a "common sense" "human rights" decision.

"This is a really important decision to hopefully rebuild a bit of trust in the reforms that came in in November," he said.

As a member also of the taskforce that reviewed the funding of aged care as part of the sector-wide reforms, Mr Symondson understood the decision to charge for showers came down to cost — although he never thought that was the right call.

"Providing fee-free or contribution-free services has a cost…and \[the government\] had wanted to make the aged care budget more manageable into the future."

Ageing Australia, OPAN and COTA were hopeful there would be further aged care announcements in the lead-up to the budget.

Their wishlist included moves to [reduce waiting times for packages and assessments,](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-08-29/waitlists-for-at-home-support-for-older-australians/105714918) and changing the assessment tool that [used an algorithm to determine someone's eligibility](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-24/aged-care-algorithm-for-home-care-under-fire/106475138) for supports at home.

"We want to see human oversight," Ms Edmond said.

The latest backflip comes after the [government caved to pressure and fast-tracked 20,000 extra home care packages](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-09-03/labor-bows-to-pressure-on-home-care-packages/105729668) to help ease the massive backlog of people waiting for help, on top of the tens of thousands more waiting for an assessment.

# Butler urged to end 'limbo' for 500,000 Australians with psychosocial disability in NDIS reforms

By senior political correspondent [Jake Evans](https://www.abc.net.au/news/jake-evans/8463902) and national health reporter [Stephanie Dalzell](https://www.abc.net.au/news/stephanie-dalzell/3318210)

- [Topic:Disabilities](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/disabilities)

1h ago1 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 2:47pm

![Two men in dark suits enter the lower house.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2544178e87573374dbc462363ab3cc44?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3098&cropW=5507&xPos=0&yPos=1&width=862&height=485)

Mark Butler is set to outline his cost-saving plan for the NDIS. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

## In short:

Mark Butler will detail his plan to save billions in spending on the disability scheme, which continues to grow faster than what the federal government says can be sustained.

Mental health groups have urged the government to maintain supports for people with psychosocial disability and provide supports for half a million Australians left in limbo.

## What's next?

Mr Butler will announce the NDIS reforms at the National Press Club today.

National Disability Insurance Scheme Minister Mark Butler must end the "limbo" half a million Australians have been living in when he announces changes to the nation's disability scheme, mental health advocates say.

The federal government is on a collision course with the states over the future of hundreds of thousands of Australians living with mental illness who are languishing outside the NDIS, as it moves to restrict access to psychosocial support through the scheme.

There are about 66,000 people on the NDIS with conditions such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, but the Productivity Commission has identified about 500,000 people who also have moderate to severe psychosocial illness but do not receive support through the NDIS or any other government-funded programs, with many state services collapsing after the NDIS was created.

In 2023 federal and state governments agreed to create services outside the NDIS to support that cohort, following the creation of a similar "foundational support" service for children with mild to moderate autism.

But little has changed since the agreement was made.

Mr Butler will address the National Press Club today, and is expected to detail changes to the NDIS that are designed to slow its growing expenses to 5 to 6 per cent a year — or more than $6 billion a year — by 2036.

The mandatory registration of providers, changes to pricing rules, limits to participant plans and tightening of Supported Independent Living are all reforms under consideration by the government.

## Anxiety over restricted access

The psychosocial disability community fears it could be a target of savings measures and that people with mental illness could be diverted from the NDIS to state services yet to be created.

Ian Hickie from the University of Sydney's Brain and Mind Centre said he anticipated that the federal government would move to ring-fence those already on the NDIS for psychosocial support, diverting access to others in need.

"There's been real anxiety that mental health or the psychosocial disability might be the sacrificial lamb on the altar of NDIS reform," he said.

![A dark-haired man in a suit sits in an office.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/88beae3c466690df1bc4ef8b28da32f0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1997&cropW=3000&xPos=0&yPos=128&width=862&height=575)

Ian Hickie says the community is anxious about the next round of reforms. (ABC News: Bryan Milliss)

A report by the Australian Psychosocial Alliance found that barriers for people with mental illnessto access the NDIS were already increasing, with people with psychosocial conditions who applied to the NDIS receiving access half as often as all other cohorts, leaving more without support over time.

Psychosocial Alliance chair Debra Zanellasaid it was time for Mr Butler to clarify how people would be supported both in and out of the NDIS.

"I would be hoping that the minister honours the commitment to investment in 'foundational supports' outside the NDIS — the recognition that there are people with psychosocial disability who belong in the scheme, for which the scheme was designed for," she said.

"And \[I hope\] that there is some articulation, today, of clarity around those people who do have a psychosocial disability and don't need to be in the scheme, that we have a very clear pathway for that support across Australia outside the NDIS.

"It is 'and' — it is not 'either-or.'

"People with a psychosocial disability do belong in the scheme … they have a long-term, permanent, functional disability that impacts on their ability to live a good life, and they require a good life."

![A dark-haired woman in a dark blazer stands on a grassy area.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cb5629fb77477e005174d2381f318ce4?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1715&cropW=2573&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Deb Zanella says there must be pathways for psychosocial supports in and out of the NDIS. (ABC News: Cason Ho)

Ms Zanella said she would be "highly surprised" if the government decided psychosocial conditions were not considered in need of NDIS support.

She noted costs for participants on the NDIS with psychosocial disability were growing within the incoming target band of 5 to 6 per cent, and there would be limited savings to be achieved by diverting that group.

Mental Health Australia chief executive Carolyn Nikoloski said supports were needed within the NDIS while also building a mental health system outside it for people with psychosocial conditions.

Without that, Ms Nikoloski said, "the strain simply shifts elsewhere".

"What we hope for is a clear and transparent plan for both how NDIS growth is to be managed within the stated targets, and how governments are finally going to deliver on their promise to deliver practical, meaningful support for the more than half a million people outside the NDIS with psychosocial disability who are currently missing out on vital mental health supports," she said.

"We have repeatedly been told that ministers take this matter seriously … yet to date we are still waiting for this solution and, with access to the NDIS potentially tightening even further, the need for substantial community-based mental health supports outside the NDIS is only going to increase."

Mr Butler would not comment on whether he would clarify supports for people with psychosocial disability in his Press Club address.

A meeting of health ministers in September is expected to progress a "foundational supports" scheme to be included as part of the next Mental Health Agreement, which is due by mid-next year.

## State supports would cost $1bn extra

But establishing a new state-based service for psychosocial disability would also set the stage for a new round of fighting between governments, which continue to argue over the Thriving Kids program to redirect children with autism to state services, despite the rapidly approaching deadline for those services to be up and running.

The Productivity Commission warned last year that state and territory expenditure would have to grow by up to $1.09 billion a year to meet the existing gaps in support and, given their limited capacity to raise revenue, "it is likely Australian government funding will be needed to support this expenditure growth".

The commission said a funding split would have to be negotiated, though states and territories should hold sole responsibility for managing psychosocial supports outside the NDIS.

Professor Hickie said today was a "critical juncture" for the NDIS and called on the Commonwealth and states to work together.

But despite the commission's recommendation that supports be established as a matter of urgency for a group "in limbo", state governments have already pushed back on a fast-tracked process, saying that beginning those reforms before the conclusion of other health and disability negotiations, such as Thriving Kids, would risk undermining the overarching system.

On Tuesday New South Wales Premier Chris Minns warned the Commonwealth not to simply shift costs onto the states.

"We can't have a situation where people are knocked off the NDIS, and then the state provides the service because it's still coming out of the same pockets of NSW taxpayers, so we have to have a sustainable system," he said.

![A middle-aged man with greying hair stands in front of a large building.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a319b5eff76687a0626b542f59ce64ee?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2849&cropW=4274&xPos=390&yPos=484&width=862&height=575)

Chris Minns is urging the federal government not to let people fall through the cracks. (AAP Image: Bianca De Marchi)

Shadow NDIS Minister Melissa McIntosh questioned how people would be supported if they lost access to the NDIS.

"If the government's answer is to push people out of the NDIS, where are they supposed to go?" she asked.

"The reality is, whether it's the NDIS, Medicare or the broader health system, the supports people need simply are not there. You either qualify for the NDIS, or you fall into the state's black hole."

Ms Zanella said if people were again left in limbo by health and disability minister "buck passing", the cost would ultimately come back to them from hospital wards.

"The reality is this will hit health ministers, in every jurisdiction because the place of last resort where these people will end up going is in our emergency departments," she said.

"It will be EDs and tertiary hospitals that will have to \[bear\] the brunt. We will simply pass the problem from one area to another area with no good outcome in between."

# States and territories left in the dark on NDIS savings

By senior political correspondent [Jake Evans](https://www.abc.net.au/news/jake-evans/8463902)

- [Topic:Disabilities](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/disabilities)

13h ago13 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 3:00am

![An NDIS logo is seen on a building](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/28a24e9b5f510e694b60f07ea07cb582?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=111&width=862&height=485)

Mark Butler will announce further reforms to the disability scheme on Wednesday. (ABC News: Patrick Stone)

## In short:

State and territory treasurers say a briefing on coming NDIS reforms was light on detail, even after criticisms that the federal government has already blindsided them once on planned savings measures.

The Queensland government has repeated its opposition to a signature savings measure that would divert children with mild and moderate autism to a new state program.

## What's next?

NDIS Minister Mark Butler will announce new savings measures for the disability scheme at the National Press Club tomorrow.

State and territory treasurers say a briefing about major reforms intended to save billions of dollars in NDIS spending was "light on detail", risking the states again being blindsided by the federal government's announcement due on Wednesday.

The government is expected to announce several measures tomorrow to curb the growing cost of the NDIS, which is forecast to double in expense to $100 billion a year by 2036 if the pace of its growth cannot be slowed.

Following a meeting between Treasurer Jim Chalmers and his state and territory counterparts, Queensland confirmed it remained opposed to a plan to divert children with autism from the NDIS to a yet-to-be-established state program — even as the government plans a new round of reforms that could divert more people with disability from the scheme.

But Mr Chalmers did not level a threat to states that they would wear the cost of NDIS overspending if they refused to adopt the $4 billion "Thriving Kids" scheme, one state source said, despite media reports.

It leaves a major NDIS savings measure unresolved even with the scheme beginning in just six months, and NDIS Minister Mark Butler due to announce a new round of reforms that will require state buy-in.

The Queensland government has resisted Thriving Kids in part because it fears the federal government's funding offer will fail to cover the costs of the program.

![A woman with short blonde hair in a navy blue suit.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/11e8fd1988b2f37d28de2e8db8d4d187?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Amanda Camm has repated her concerns that Thriving Kids will not sufficiently support children with mild to moderate autism. (AAP: Darren England)

Following the briefing, Queensland Disability Services Minister Amanda Camm repeated the QLD government's position that the Commonwealth was shirking its responsibilities.

"The federal government's plan to walk away from their responsibilities to children and families is failing kids, not thriving kids," Ms Camm said.

"Serious concerns remain about the long-term care of these children — let alone those who are next in the crosshairs of their NDIS failure to plan."

Treasurers said there was "not a lot of detail" from Mr Chalmers and Mr Butler, positive or negative, on the NDIS reforms due to be announced at the National Press Club tomorrow, leaving them on a "watching brief" alongside the public to see what the Commonwealth had planned.

That is despite criticism by state premiers that they were blindsided in August last year, when Mr Butler first laid out the government's intention to curb spending growth of the NDIS to 5 to 6 per cent a year primarily by shifting responsibility of services for children with mild and moderate autism to the states.

Shadow NDIS Minister Melissa McIntosh said Mr Chalmers had "war-gamed" billions of dollars of cuts "behind closed doors".

"For people with disabilities and their families … they are clinging to tomorrow's Press Club speech by Health Minister Mark Butler, hoping for some clarity on how these decisions will impact them. Because right now, they have none," Ms McIntosh said in a statement.

## States fear carrying the can on NDIS

Despite limited details, West Australian Treasurer Rita Saffioti said she wanted to remain cooperative with the federal government.

"I think that discussion \[on cost-sharing\] has been had. I would say the NDIS was something that was created by the federal government, we have seen some of that management be not as effective as we would like," Ms Saffioti said.

"\[But\] I'd rather try to work cooperatively."

![A woman with a beige suit speaks to reporters](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/39fc033a73de4b7eb2fc1a8e7d24a385?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Rita Saffioti says there is not a lot of detail in the NDIS briefing from the Commonwealth. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

NSW Premier Chris Minns said he did not want to "jump at shadows" and would work with the Commonwealth in good faith.

But he warned against removing people from the NDIS if it risked leaving them without necessary supports.

"We can't have a situation where people are knocked off the NDIS, and then the state provides the service, because it's still coming out of the same pockets of NSW taxpayers," Mr Minns said.

"We want to do our bit. We recognise the need for reform but ultimately we want to make sure people don't slip through the cracks."

Ms Saffioti confirmed a widely anticipated expanded registration scheme for NDIS providers would also be part of tomorrow's reform announcement.

The vast majority of NDIS providers are able to offer taxpayer-funded supports without going through a formal vetting process, which has exposed the $50 billion scheme to claims of rorting and predation on vulnerable participants.

State disability ministers will receive a briefing on the NDIS reforms following Mr Butler's speech.

The next round of cost-saving measures for the NDIS has been flagged by Mr Chalmers as the single largest savings measure in the May federal budget.

The government wants to bring the pace of growth of the NDIS in line with aged care, health and other social service spending — down from its current rate of about 9.5 per cent to 5 to 6 per cent a year.

Doing so would net the government an additional $6 billion a year or more by a decade's time, while if spending growth is not slowed the national disability program would double in size by 2036 to become the single most expensive Commonwealth program.

## Disability campaign opposes 'cuts to ordinary lives'

Ahead of Wednesday's announcement, People with Disability Australia has launched a campaign opposing cuts to the NDIS, saying they would be "cuts to ordinary lives".

PwDA says for every $1 invested into NDIS funding, $2.25 was contributed to the economy, while the scheme enabled participants to work, study and participate in community life.

Its president, Jeramy Hope, said NDIS funding was reasonable and necessary.

"I rely on about 25 to 30 hours of support each week. That support is not a luxury. It is what allows me to work, spend time with my family and stay out of hospital," Mr Hope said in a statement.

"When governments talk about reducing growth, they are talking about cutting the supports people rely on to live ordinary lives. For people like me, the NDIS is what allows us to get out of bed, shower safely and be part of our communities."

The group urged government to focus on fixing inefficiencies in the system rather than cutting eligibility.

# Queensland Tourism Industry Council calls on government for support as tourists cancel bookings

- [Topic:Tourism and Leisure Industry](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/tourism-and-leisure-industry)

11m ago11 minutes agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 4:48am

![A man stands on a road in a caravan park looking into the distance.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3bc78b3675083141c4822d0f3137cb85?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1650&cropW=2934&xPos=0&yPos=439&width=862&height=485)

Besko Halilovic says annual visitors to Far North Queensland are cancelling their trips due to concerns over fuel. (ABC News: Christopher Testa)

## In short:

The Queensland Tourism Industry Council is calling for the state and federal governments to help businesses with cash flow and retaining their workforce over the next 90 days.

Caravan Parks Association of Queensland chief executive Michelle Weston says operators rely on tourists visiting in the winter months to keep them running right through summer.

A spokesperson for federal minister Don Farrel says the government is "taking every practical step to shield Australians from the worst impacts of the conflict in the Middle East".

The manager of a Far North Queensland caravan park says they have lost about $35,000 in bookings due to concerns over fuel prices and availability, as the industry calls for state and federal government support for operators.

Besko Halilovic, who started as manager at Crystal Cascades Caravan Park almost two months ago, said more people were ringing up to cancel than to book a spot.

"It seems like this season is ruined," he said. "That's how it feels."

He said the number of people citing fuel as the reason for cancelling had led staff to create a special category to track the impact it was having.

![A man wearing glasses sits looking at a computer with the top of the screen just in frame.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cceb43643e069b5ee4671923180da7cb?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2688&cropW=4032&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Besko Halilovic says when cancelling their bookings, people tell him they can not afford the fuel for their driving holiday. (ABC News: Christopher Testa)

The Queensland Tourism Industry Council (QTIC) is calling for a support package worth about $50 million from the state and federal governments to help businesses with cash flow and retaining their workforce over the next 90 days.

"We are looking at an industry that is trying to keep their staff retained for when people are travelling in volume again," QTIC chief executive Natassia Wheeler said.

"If you lose that workforce, how do they remain open seven days a week and accept people and deliver on the expectations?"

![A woman looking concerned talks a a person in a blue top slightly out of camera](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6842bf8971e8a694912a2350b0a19c9b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1833&cropW=2750&xPos=1282&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Natassia Wheeler says the industry is trying to keep staff retained for when business picks up again. (ABC News: Lucy Loram)

According to QTIC, the industry supplies more than 277,000 jobs and $43 billion of visitor cashflow across Queensland, with regional destinations most effected by transport dependencies.

## Concerns during 'core months'

Caravan Parks Association of Queensland chief executive Michelle Weston said operators relied on tourists visiting in the winter months to keep them running right through summer.

![A woman with short spikey blond hair ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cd98a82642035afe12da564bf7081eac?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2268&cropW=3402&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Michelle Weston says operators in north Queensland lose money during the summer. (ABC News: Lucy Loram)

"Outside south-east Queensland, most caravan parks in Queensland actually run cashflow negative through summer, which means they earn all of their income through those winter months," she said.

Chrizelle Zwick, who manages Lake Placid Rainforest Retreat in Far North Queensland, said the "fuel dilemma couldn't have happened at a worse time".

![A woman wearing a teal polo walking down a road in a caravan park.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/343cdc9f41d8f8543960ef9f79c14478?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2688&cropW=4032&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Chrizelle Zwick wants the government to reassure the public about fuel supply. (ABC News: Christopher Testa)

"June, July and August — those three months are what prop us up for what carry us through the rest of the year," she said.

"\[They are\] our core months that keep us alive, so if we're sort of having problems in those months it's really significant damage to the business."

## Information 'the ace up the sleeve'

Sydneysiders Kerrie Murphy and Carin Vernier are on a dream retirement lap of Australia but have had to rethink their itinerary due to concerns around fuel availability.

![Two women smile sitting side by side in caming chairs outside.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/50dfbaa6e84c8f7dc9631aedbffd6ab1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1134&cropW=1701&xPos=158&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Kerrie Murphy and Carin Vernier are rethinking their dream retirement holiday due to concerns around fuel availability. (ABC News: Russel Talbot)

"At this stage, we're probably only going to get as far as north Queensland," Ms Murphy said.

They are cautious of travelling to remote locations as they are concerned about the availability of fuel.

"We definitely decided that we would just stick to the coast where hopefully fuel availability was good," Ms Vernier said.

To give both tourism and operators certainty, Queensland Bus Industry Council executive director Jason O'Dwyer would like the federal government to create a publicly accessible fuel dashboard.

![A man in a suit](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/251c8b220f0bdeaaba51b40989869974?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1584&cropW=2376&xPos=1312&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Jason O'Dwyer says public information on fuel availability would give everyone confidence. (ABC News: Lucy Loram)

This comes after Premier David Crisafulli called on the federal government last month for a national fuel dashboard.

Mr O'Dwyer said having information on supply publicly available would give confidence to both operators and tourists.

"In a crisis, cash is king, however the ace up the sleeve is information," Mr O'Dwyer said.

"It's really important that organisations have an idea that they can do tours and then go out and come back and bring passengers home."

Caravan park operator Murray McLay said bookings had softened across his two venues in Yeppoon and Keppel Sands.

"At this stage, it's a wait-and-see; we're certainly down on income with the cancellations," he said.

![A man in a long sleeve polo leans against a tree while smiling in a caravan park.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/97ef0baa7e3cb79515591de82d0d8fe9?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1134&cropW=1701&xPos=158&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Murray McLay would like to see more certainty around fuel supply. (ABC News: Russel Talbot)

Mr McLay said he was confident that the industry could weather the storm, but that a national fuel dashboard could help ease travellers' anxieties.

"They're not worried about if it's $2.40 or $2.70. It's, 'if I go to the servo, I want to be able to fuel up'."

A spokesperson for federal Tourism Minister Don Farrell said tourism businesses were the "heartbeat of many towns and communities".

"The government is coordinating closely with state and territory governments and taking every practical step to shield Australians from the worst impacts of the conflict in the Middle East," they said.

# Government invests in counter-drone technology citing lessons of Iran war

By acting defence and national security correspondent [Tom Lowrey](https://www.abc.net.au/news/tom-lowrey/7137788)

- [Topic:Defence and National Security](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/defence-and-national-security)

5h ago5 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 2:25am

![A side profile of Pat Conroy as he speaks next to Richard Marles.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/afdc2e8e38f8aca467d097e8db76ec68?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3510&cropW=6240&xPos=0&yPos=13&width=862&height=485)

Pat Conroy says it is vital Australia is not caught relying on expensive missiles to shoot down cheap drones. (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

## In short:

The federal government will invest $30 million in two Australian companies specialising in counter-drone measures.

One company, Sypaq, is developing an armed drone designed to target larger drones, while AIM Defence is developing a high-powered anti-drone laser.

## What's next?

The funding comes as part of $7 billion the government says will be invested in counter-drone measures over the next 10 years.

The federal government has signed contracts with two Australian companies specialising in counter-drone technologies, as it says it is learning lessons from conflicts abroad.

The two contracts, worth a combined $30 million, are a small slice of $7 billion in new funding set aside for counter-drone options over the next decade.

Widescale use of cheap, mass-produced drones has been a dominant feature of the war in Ukraine and the current Iran conflict.

One contract has been signed with Australian company Sypaq, which has [previously supplied cheap cardboard drones to the Ukrainian military](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-06-28/pro-putin-military-blogger-claims-australian-built-drone-crashed/102530180).

The drones — which were supplied as part of an Australian support package to Ukraine — have been used in attacks on Russian airfields, damaging aircraft.

The $10.4 million contract will help fund the development of Sypaq's Corvo Strike drone, which tracks, targets and destroys larger drones.

Another $21.3 million in funding will go to AIM Defence to fund its Fractl high-powered laser, capable of tracking small, fast-moving objects and burning through steel.

The laser can track an object the size of a 10 cent piece, travelling at more than 100 kilometres per hour.

![A small grey drone sits on a table with Sypaq banner in the background.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/81b19b17a8016868a8bfc5a8ba1f0f38?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1707&cropW=2560&xPos=0&yPos=239&width=862&height=575)

Sypaq's Corvo Strike drone tracks, targets and destroys larger drones. (Supplied: Sypaq)

Australian defence investment in drone technology has previously concentrated on expensive, highly capable platforms such as the [Ghost Bat uncrewed aircraft and Ghost Shark uncrewed submarine](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-18/australia-military-drone-warfare-push-ghost-bat-ghost-shark/106563072).

But many analysts have pointed to the extensive use of cheap, mass-produced drones in both the Ukraine and Iran conflicts as providing important lessons.

Defence Industry Minister Pat Conroy said it was vital Australia was not caught relying on expensive missiles to shoot down cheap drones, as has been seen elsewhere.

"These systems are cheap. Cheap but highly advanced," he said.

"You don't want to be in a position long term — and this is common sense — to have to fire $3 million missiles to take out a $100,000 drone.

"These systems are in the tens of thousands of dollars."

## Aiming for asymmetric advantage

The extensive use of Iran's Shahed drone in attacks on its regional neighbours, particularly the United Arab Emirates and Qatar, has captured significant attention.

In the early days of the conflict, Iran fired hundreds of armed drones daily at targets across both countries.

That barrage drained the UAE, Qatar and the United States' supply of interceptors, and saw expensive Patriot missiles worth around $4 million used to take them down.

The Shahed drone is estimated to cost $30,000 to manufacture.

Some defence analysts point out that spending $4 million to shoot down a $30,000 drone can be worth it if it is protecting human life or a valuable target.

But they say sustainability is the long-term concern.

The latest National Defence Strategy, [released last week](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-15/defence-spend-53b-next-decade-intensifying-risks-richard-marles/106568890), points to this area as an immediate priority for the Australian Defence Force.

And it points to the sector as a significant export opportunity, as militaries around the world learn the same lesson at the same time.

Mr Conroy said they were alive to that possibility.

"All the capabilities that we are developing are options that we might consider for export," he said.

"We lead the world in autonomous systems, both at a large scale and at a small scale, and we're open — subject to stringent defence export controls — to provide them to allies and like-minded partners."

Mr Conroy was also asked if the ADF was interested in developing and acquiring significant numbers of cheap, long-range guided drones, similar to the Shahed.

"I'm not going to comment on that specifically because we've got a number of capabilities that are being developed right now that it's not in the national interest to disclose," he said.

"But I can assure Australians that we've got some of the most advanced drones and autonomous systems in the world across a range of capabilities, but for obvious reasons, we don't disclose all of them."

# Carpooling helps to slash fuel prices, so why aren't more people doing it?

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3363d1fe015667dc9e3bdf99fac1c425?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3369&cropW=3369&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
M\\
\\
By Monty Jacka](https://www.abc.net.au/news/monty-jacka/102206016)
- [Topic:Transport](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/transport)

4h ago4 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 3:27pm

![Woman leaning on car.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d168c4d9483d474523e7cde3759bd431?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=876&cropW=1557&xPos=1&yPos=200&width=862&height=485)

Blanche Sayers is about to start carpooling with her colleague to save money on petrol. (ABC News: Jake Grant)

## In short:

Carpooling is one of the "travel alternatives" being promoted by the Australian government during the current fuel crisis.

While it is hard to say how many people are adopting the behaviour, the limited information available suggests it has not been significant.

## What's next?

Workplace programs, digital matching platforms, and targeted incentives are being touted as ways to increase carpooling rates.

Frustrated by the current petrol prices, primary school teacher Blanche Sayers is about to start carpooling with a colleague.

They both live in the Hobart suburb of Mount Nelson and have given each other lifts before, but never considered regular carpooling until the current fuel crisis.

> "It just seemed like a sensible solution to save a little bit of money,"

Ms Sayers said.

Carpooling is one of the "travel alternatives" being promoted by the Australian government as part of [the national fuel security plan](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-30/four-step-fuel-supply-plan-national-cabinet-fuel-crisis/106512706).

In a list of fuel-saving tips, the Department of Infrastructure suggested "getting to a destination with a few friends nearby could be a great way to do your bit".

But while some people, like Ms Sayers, have managed to embrace shared commutes quite easily — others have found it more difficult.

![Two men filling up their cars with petrol at a Shell service station in Hobart.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6769ef42712d206d1006b80022ff788d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=5952&cropW=8928&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The federal Department of Infrastructure has suggested carpooling as a way for people to save money on petrol. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)

## Increase of just 1 per cent in Sydney carpooling lane

Multiple people contacted for this story said they were unable to find anyone to carpool with, despite reaching out to their community.

There is also very limited data available to track whether more people are adopting the behaviour.

Transit lanes, typically signposted as T2 or T3, are designated lanes restricted to vehicles carrying multiple passengers, designed to promote carpooling.

New South Wales is the only state or territory that tracks the number of vehicles using the lanes, which are also available to electric vehicles and public transport.

According to Transport for NSW, about 206,000 vehicles used the T3 lane on Spit Road, heading towards the Sydney CBD, on weekday mornings in February.

That figure rose to about 209,000 in March — an increase of just 1 per cent — despite the start of the current Middle East conflict causing higher fuel prices.

![A mix of cars and trucks fill four lanes of peak hour traffic on freeway.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/05d4591eef29ec50e9eed723c72520d8?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1333&cropW=1999&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

There is limited information available to measure the uptake of carpooling in Australia. (ABC News: Andrew O'Connor)

## Australians struggle to embrace carpooling, expert says

The perceived lack of uptake is unsurprising for Hussein Dia, who is a professor of Transport, Technology and Sustainability at Swinburne University.

He said carpooling in Australia had "never picked up" despite concerted efforts from the sustainability sector over the past four decades.

"In theory, it is very effective because people are sharing a vehicle between two or three people, which can reduce the fuel use and cost per person," Professor Dia said.

> "People want to do it and they're keen to do it, but the underlying systems and support systems are not there."

![A photo of a man in a navy suit standing in front of a road network map with the Swinburne University logo.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d124f2bff04a46eb7539282f2a97444b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Hussein Dia says carpooling has "never picked up" in Australia, despite efforts from the sustainability sector. (Supplied: Hussein Dia)

The census in 2021, conducted during the COVID pandemic, found that of the roughly 6.8 million responders who travelled to work in a car, only 6 per cent were passengers.

Professor Dia said the key hurdle for most Australians was finding someone whose schedule aligned for the commute both to and from work.

"People have different work schedules and nowadays we even have hybrid days, so people's schedules in many cases are not aligned," he said.

## Awareness campaign 'unlikely' to drive widespread uptake

If the federal government were serious about increasing carpooling rates, Professor Dia said the focus would need to shift to establishing enabling conditions.

He said this could include workplace-based coordination, digital matching platforms, and targeted incentives that make shared travel more attractive.

> "Awareness campaigns on their own are unlikely to drive widespread uptake \[of carpooling\],"

Professor Dia said.

"One of the most practical approaches is workplace coordination … where employers actively help staff to organise shared travel, rather than leaving it to individuals.

Discounts on toll roads and free parking at workplaces were other incentives that he suggested could help drive more people to share their commute.

He said digital platforms that match together commuters looking to head in the same direction were a growing trend that could prove effective in major cities.

![A man with grey hair and a beard sitting in the front seat of a vehicle.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4ceff4bd16b33c77faae4ff97ab46710?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Francois Chemillier started developing his app after seeing the number of single-occupant cars stuck in traffic in Sydney's northern beaches. (Supplied: Francois Chemillier)

## Carpooling should be promoted beyond fuel crisis, app developer says

Francois Chemillier, who lives in Sydney's Northern Beaches, is part of this wave — having just launched his own carpooling app.

He was inspired to pull the platform together two years ago, due to the number of single-occupant vehicles stuck in traffic each day near his home.

> "\[Carpooling\] is really the most effective solution right now for Australians to adopt, to limit the use of fuel,"

Mr Chemillier said.

"But without the proper technology, you won't easily find someone who goes where you want to go, the same day at the same time."

![A middle-aged woman with shoulder length blonde hair sitting at the front wheel of a car.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ca0f54d65d06e81dcd9983fd844db523?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1152&cropW=1728&xPos=320&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

As well as saving money, Blanche Sayers says she's looking forward to having company during her commute. (ABC News: Jake Grant)

Mr Chemillier was hopeful that digital platforms like his could be key in making carpooling more accessible and helping to drive behavioural change.

He said the government should continue to promote the practice beyond the current fuel crisis, due to its environmental and social benefits.

Beyond simply saving money, Ms Sayers said she felt her plans to start carpooling with her colleague would also provide a positive social outlet.

"It will be nice just travelling with a colleague and having somebody to talk to in the morning and afternoon," she said.

# ACCC says Woolworths 'contravened' own guardrails for 'Prices Dropped' promotion, court hears

By national consumer affairs reporter [Michael Atkin](https://www.abc.net.au/news/michael-atkin/5778984) and the Specialist Reporting Team's [Lucy Kent](https://www.abc.net.au/news/lucy-kent/11566898)

- [Topic:Consumer Protection](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/consumer-protection)

19h ago19 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 1:03am

![Wideshot of Woolworths sign on the building with tree in foreground, generic.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cf6a13b3b6359affc682edb354bd0782?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1971&cropW=3504&xPos=0&yPos=183&width=862&height=485)

Woolworths will be defending the case brought against it by ACCC, arguing cost increases were driven by suppliers and the discounts were real. (ABC News: Billy Cooper)

During day one of a federal court case, the consumer watchdog has alleged Woolworths "contravened" its own internal policies when placing products on its "Prices Dropped" program.

Both the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) and Woolworths gave opening statements that were punctuated by lengthy exchanges with the judge about what was relevant.

The case is similar to [one the ACCC pursued against Coles in February](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-02-16/accc-coles-federal-court-case-discount-pricing/106294956), which has already gone to trial and is awaiting a highly anticipated judgment.

Both supermarket giants are being accused of misleading consumers by intentionally increasing a product's price for a short period, before discounting them to a price that was more than, or the same as, the initial price.

The ACCC claimed Woolworths shoppers were not receiving genuine discounts on hundreds of items.

Michael Hodge KC, who is representing the consumer watchdog, said the 266 products in question contravened internal Woolworths guidelines known as guardrails.

"If these guardrails, as they existed in 2020, had been applied in relation to the products that we're concerned with, then all of them would have contravened the price-establishment period guideline," he said.

![A man in court dress and glasses carries documents as he walks into court.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/8d3ff53133c4afa144e18b7af22b7288?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=958&cropW=1437&xPos=0&yPos=1&width=862&height=575)

Michael Hodge KC is representing ACCC in its case against Woolworths. (ABC News: Billy Cooper)

The price establishment guideline is the length of time a product should remain on a price before being reduced.

On Tuesday afternoon, Woolworths challenged the ACCC’s interpretation of its guardrails and its alleged non-compliance with it.

Woolworths lead counsel Robert Yezerski SC said internal guidelines confirmed it had a compliance program that showed it took the law seriously.

"Woolworths reads and takes seriously ACCC guidance," Mr Yezerski said.

## The 'power' of the promotion

Mr Hodge argued the "power" of the "Prices Dropped" marketing message was that it conveyed to a shopper that Woolworths had "done something remarkable or unusual".

"It has dropped the regular shelf price," he said.

> "The subtle magic of the prices dropped message, that draws the consumer in, is … that the new stable price is lower than the old stable price,"

he said.

He used the example of the Oreo Family Pack Original ticket, which showed the product at the price of $5 on December 19, 2022, then dropped to $4.50.

Oreo Family Pack Original 370g

### Oreo Family Pack Original 370g

The chart shows the product has a Price Dropped tag at $3.50 for two years. The price rises to $5 for a very short period of time before dropping again to $4.50. This is $1 more than the Price Dropped price of $3.50 just a month earlier.

$3.00$4.00$5.00

White ticket $5.00

Prices Dropped $3.50

Prices Dropped $4.50

He explained from January 2021 to the end of November 2022, the item was being sold at less than $4.

"Then there's a brief period of a few weeks when the product goes up to $5, and then after a few weeks the price drops back down to $4.50, and is now described as Prices Dropped," he told the court.

He argued consumers would form the view that based on the price history of the product "that the ticket was misleading or false".

![A red and white label saying Prices Dropped for Oreos Family Pack.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/052f8631a541c19963a4fad43deda50d?src)

The price of the Oreo Family Pack spiked to $5.00 for 22 days before Woolworths put it on a "Prices Dropped" price of $4.50. (Supplied: ACCC)

There was also a lot of back and forth between Mr Hodge and Justice Michael O'Bryan about what an average consumer would think about when they saw the "Prices Dropped" tickets.

Justice O'Bryan suggested "consumers wouldn't over-intellectualise these tickets at all".

"They wouldn't be thinking in the terms that you put to me in the first few minutes of this opening session, but at a much more general level … that what was flagged on that ticket was some kind of genuine discount," he said.

Justice O'Bryan said Mr Hodge would have to persuade him that in the "10 seconds" a consumer decided to buy a product after seeing the Prices Dropped signage, that they were thinking more than, "Woolworths is telling me that there is a real and genuine discount from that price to that price".

"Perhaps Mr Hodges, when you're shopping in Woolworths there might be more analysis going on," he laughed.

"But I suspect for most consumers it doesn't go much beyond that."

## Woolworths 'largely removed' guardrails

Mr Hodge told the court Woolworths had changed the way they implemented their "Prices Dropped" policy at the beginning of 2022 because they were trying to put more products onto the popular promotional program.

He also referenced an email from a pricing manager to staff members which mentioned the introduction of a "new price establishment period of three to six weeks" around this time.

He alleged at the time Woolworths had "largely removed their guardrails" and were only setting lower shelf prices for some products so they could increase it when it went on the Prices Dropped promotion.

![Shoppers at the self check-out at a Woolworths store, generic.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/343999e7fb1d36cfbcf55d4100dc2bfc?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2336&cropW=3504&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The consumer watchdog claims Woolworths prevented shoppers from making informed choices during the relevant period. (ABC News: Billy Cooper)

"That 'was' price is only being put in place in order to establish it for the purposes of them being used in the \[Prices Dropped\] ticket."

In response, Mr Yezerski rejected the ACCC's claims, telling the court his client had provided "ample evidence" of genuine prices.

Mr Yezerski said the products had been sold in significant quantities at the higher prices before being on a "Prices Dropped" promotion.

He also said suppliers had requested cost increases in almost all of the cases.

The hearing will resume on Wednesday.

# Vanuatu denies it is about to sign a security deal with China as Australia works to ink its own pact

By foreign affairs correspondent [Stephen Dziedzic](https://www.abc.net.au/news/stephen-dziedzic/166934) and Vanuatu reporter [Lillyrose Welwel](https://www.abc.net.au/news/lillyrose-welwel/105007524)

- [Topic:World Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/world-politics)

2h ago2 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 3:21am

![Two men in suits shaking hands ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/5b77bd4593493cdd19eb759a04be3e4d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=900&cropW=1600&xPos=0&yPos=150&width=862&height=485)

Jotham Napat and Anthony Albanese are yet to formalise the Nakamal Agreement between the two countries despite months of negotiations. (ABC News: Lillyrose Welwel, file)

## In short:

Vanuatu's prime minister has dismissed reports his country is on the verge of signing a security agreement with China.

Jotham Napat said reports suggesting as such were "untrue" before adding that his country's relationships "were not exclusive".

Australia's Pacific Minister Pat Conroy would not confirm if the federal government had concerns over Vanuatu's potential deal with China.

Vanuatu has furiously denied that it is on the cusp of signing a security pact with China and warned that it will not be "dictated to" by Australia in a row which threatens to disrupt delicately poised negotiations for the landmark Nakamal Agreement.

Multiple sources have confirmed that China and Vanuatu have spent months discussing a comprehensive new pact called the Namele Agreement.

In Vanuatu, the Namele leaf is a sacred symbol of peace and authority used to enforce kastom or customary laws.

On Tuesday morning the Vanuatu newspaper the Daily Post reported that Australian officials in Port Vila were concerned the deal could have security elements, quoting a source who said it might "affect agreements with Australia" and undermine Vanuatu's push to make Canberra ease visa restrictions.

But later that day Vanuatu's Prime Minister Jotham Napat issued a forceful statement calling the report "grossly misguided and inaccurate" and insisting the proposed agreement was a "comprehensive development cooperation agreement" not a security pact.

He also took a thinly veiled swipe at Australia saying that "Vanuatu will decide what is good for Vanuatu. We will not be dictated to."

> "Our foreign policy is guided by our national interests, not by external speculation or pressure,"

Mr Napat said.

"Suggestions that our partnerships could affect visa arrangements or other bilateral matters are unhelpful and not a cultural reflection of Nakamal values.

"Vanuatu's relationships are not exclusive."

## Airing of concerns 'serious' amid negotiations

The dispute risks poisoning the atmosphere between Australia and Vanuatu just as the two countries prepare to finalise protracted and sometimes difficult negotiations over the Nakamal Agreement — a landmark security and development pact between the two countries.

Last week Vanuatu's Climate Minister Ralph Regenvanu said the government was on the "cusp" of finally signing the deal, almost eight months after it was given initial approval on the summit of Mt Yasur.

![Six people sit behind a table signing a document with smoke rising from a crater behind them](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/fa6c85e137298932657811304c107ce9?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2232&cropW=3348&xPos=162&yPos=506&width=862&height=575)

Senior ministers from Australia and Vanuatu initialled the Nakamal Agreement at the top of Mount Yasur, an active volcano on the island of Tanna, in August last year. (ABC News: Lillyrose WelWel)

One diplomat in Vanuatu from a third country said the fact Australian officials still pressed ahead with raising public concerns about the pact with China, albeit by an anonymous leak to a newspaper, showed they must have "serious" concerns that it would have security implications.

The ABC has been told that Australia is not opposed to Vanuatu building up its economic links with China, but wants to ensure that the Namele pact is not a security agreement which might undermine Nakamal, or threaten Australia's interests.

The Pacific Minister Pat Conroy would not confirm if Australia had concerns, or say whether officials in Vanuatu had tried to use the Namele agreement as leverage to secure a better deal in negotiations on the Nakamal Agreement.

But he said Australia was in a "daily contest" with unnamed other countries throughout the Pacific.

"We've been very honest with the Australian public that we're in a permanent state of contest for influence in the Pacific," Mr Conroy said.

"That's why we've put so much resources and so much ministerial energy into this area."

## Chinese influence in region monitored closely

Australia has also been closely monitoring meetings between the Chinese government and Vanuatu government ministers, including Interior Affairs Minister Andrew Napuat, who went to a major summit on digital technology in Hong Kong last week.

Mr Napat said the conference was focused on "addressing challenges in cyber security, digital transformation, and emerging technologies" and a senior government source in Vanuatu denied outright that any agreements had been signed with China during his trip.

The Chinese embassy in Vanuatu also said the claim of a security agreement was "untrue" and that "China–Vanuatu cooperation focuses on infrastructure development and capacity building".

![A grader lays bitumen for a new road. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/cac2ec1aadc485e44088607e2a73b4cc?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1365&cropW=2048&xPos=0&yPos=85&width=862&height=575)

China has loaned money to Vanuatu financing new roads for its outer islands in recent years. (Facebook: China Civil Engineering Construction Corporation, file)

Mr Napat also insisted the discussions around finalising the Nakamal Agreement with Australia were "not at all linked to China".

"Officials are continuing close engagement to ensure any agreement with any partner reflects Vanuatu's interests as high priority in ensuring its longevity," he said.

The agreement hit the rocks just before it was due to be signed in Port Vila by prime ministers [Anthony Albanese and Jotham Napat in September,](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-09-09/albanase-vanuatu-australia-nakamal-agreement/105750522) with Vanuatu deciding it could not endorse language aimed at limiting China's security presence in the Pacific.

That included a section emphasising that Pacific nations should have clear responsibility for security matters in this region, and another section which was designed to limit foreign investment in critical infrastructure in Vanuatu.

Even before this dispute there has been evidence of tensions between the two sides.

Multiple sources in Vanuatu have said Australian diplomats had deliberately kept an unusually low profile in recent weeks, a move one official said was "strange" and noticeable".

Mr Conroy would not be drawn on when the Nakamal Agreement was set to be signed.

"We've had very productive conversations but you'll hear about an announcement when we make it," he said.

Shadow Foreign Minister Ted O'Brien said Australia was "deeply invested in its relationship with Vanuatu" and the government needed to make it built a "strong, respectful and enduring partnership".

"The Albanese government has questions to answer here," Mr O'Brien said.

"What is the status of the Nakamal Agreement? And what steps are being taken to encourage its finalisation?

"This is looking like yet another diplomatic blunder on the part of the Albanese government and given the strategic importance of this relationship I expect Labor will exhaust every effort to improve the situation."

# China opens the gate to more Aussie beef with new export licences for several facilities

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/184f1c0dd2336a1bddd79ac79a353659?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1120&cropW=1120&xPos=754&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
M\\
\\
By Matt Brann](https://www.abc.net.au/news/rural/matt-brann/4580082)
- [ABC Rural](https://www.abc.net.au/news/rural/)
- [Topic:Trade](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/trade)

5h ago5 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 2:21am

![Chef cooking beef with China and Australian flags in the background](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ef08860d3a56df67c28ee69b5735e409?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

More Australian beef looks set to be exported to China. (Supplied: MLA/ABC Landline)

### In short:

China has approved access to several meat businesses in Australia to start exporting beef.

Meat analyst Simon Quilty says the timing is unusual, given Australia is set to reach its beef quota to China by mid-June.

China's Ministry of Agriculture has confirmed there have been foot-and-mouth disease outbreaks in the country and animals have been culled.

China has granted export licences to eight new Australian facilities to start supplying beef, just months after introducing a range of trade measures and tariffs to protect its domestic cattle industry.

China's customs website (GACC) shows late on Friday (April 17), China approved access to six distribution centres (cold stores) and also two abattoirs — including the Thomas Foods International meatworks near Murray Bridge in South Australia.

The federal Department of Agriculture has confirmed the new allocations and said China had also given 13 China-listed abattoirs approval to allow additional product to be exported.

Global AgriTrends's meat analyst, Simon Quilty, said the new approvals plus the "upgrading" of 13 export licences, would allow Australia to export more chilled beef to China.

"So I understand those 13 licences have been upgraded from frozen to chilled beef into China as well," he said.

"So the two new abattoirs, plus 13, gives a total of 15 new extra licences for chilled beef to China, which is more than double what Australia has had in the past."

In a statement, Agriculture Minister Julie Collins said, "additional market access is welcomed and highlights the high quality and safe beef that Australia exports".

![Australian beef for sale in China](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/24ec6f149ac18e1ba1b5aa20672a8d4e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2177&cropW=3264&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

According to MLA, China imported 272,940 tonnes of beef from Australia in 2025.  (Craig Zonca)

## What's driving the sudden demand?

Mr Quilty said the timing of China's decision was unusual.

He said at the start of this year, China enforced a [205,000-tonne quota on Australian beef](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-01-01/china-hits-australian-beef-industry-with-tariff/106191726) imports, which was on track to be filled by mid-June.

Any beef exports from Australia after that would face an unviable 55 per cent tariff.

He said there had been reports of outbreaks of foot-and-mouth disease (FMD) in China and wondered if the severity of the outbreaks was being downplayed.

"Australia has been given all of this extra access, but we're going to hit the \[205,000-tonne quota\] within a very short period of time, it really doesn't make sense unless there's other changes afoot," he said.

![a cow's mouth with a blister from foot and mouth disease](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/5978c3afff47a997ecb9f89d9d879f8b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=300&cropW=450&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Australia does not have foot-and-mouth disease. (Supplied: Agriculture Victoria)

In late March, China's Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs [confirmed outbreaks](https://xmsyj.moa.gov.cn/yqfb/202603/t20260329_6482767.htm) of FMD in Yining County, Xinjiang and also in Gulang County, Gansu Province — which are about 2,400 kilometres apart.

The brief statement said 142 of the 513 cattle at the trading market in Xinjang were "sick" and one farm in Gulang County, Gansu Province had 77 cattle sick out of 5,716 head.

> "After the outbreak, the local government immediately carried out culling, cleaning and disinfection,"

it said.

It said authorities were monitoring the situation "in accordance with the requirement of the emergency plan".

But Mr Quilty said non-state media outlets were reporting FMD outbreaks involving the SAT-1 strain, were happening in other regions of China.

"If China is suffering domestic losses of fresh beef due to FMD, plus a [lack of access for American beef](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-05-24/shanghai-expo-during-trump-tariffs-puts-aussie-beef-centre-stage/105326724), it points to a lack of chilled beef in China and therefore giving 15 new licences \[for Australian exports makes sense\].

> "These allocations are possibly an early indicator of China changing their structure and hopefully giving Australia much greater access,"

he said

Meat industry representatives have told ABC Rural that China's increased demand for Australian beef was good news, but they would not comment on "theories" that it may be driven by FMD outbreaks in China, and that many nations were looking to improve their food security during the current global uncertainty.

ABC Rural has sought comment from Thomas Foods International and the Australian Meat Industry Council.

# More uncounted votes discovered in SA a month after state election

- [Topic:State and Territory Elections](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/state-and-territory-elections)

17h ago17 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 2:13am

![A ballot box during an election.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f36eb9388c250b077c1bdb5f19a340e3?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=284&width=862&height=485)

The Electoral Commission said the newly discovered ballots were from the seats of Enfield and Newland. (ABC News: Trent Murphy)

## In short:

More uncounted ballot papers from last month's state election have been found, the Electoral Commission of SA has confirmed.

It said the votes were for both houses of parliament and were from the electorates of Enfield and Newland.

## What's next?

The SA government has since reaffirmed its commitment to an independent review of the election and ECSA's running of it.

More uncounted ballots have been discovered a month on from the South Australian state election — the second such instance in less than a week.

The ballot papers, for both the upper and lower houses of state parliament, are from the electorates of Enfield and Newland, the Electoral Commission of SA (ECSA) said.

Both seats were safely won by Labor, and ECSA said the number of votes "would not have affected any House of Assembly seat".

But in a statement it did not confirm how many had been found, other than saying they amounted to a "small number".

ECSAsaid the newly discovered upper house ballots would be "included in the Legislative Council count, which is due to be finalised on 1 May".

In the statement, Acting Electoral Commissioner Leah McLay said the commission's performance at the recent election had fallen "well short" of community expectations.

> "While the operational issues that have occurred have not affected the integrity of the election, I share the community's frustration with these unacceptable system failures,"

she said.

![A woman with blonde hair and black glasses wearing a black jacket and top inside](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d6f0e1ed2dad8965b5d2f813c55ea48e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=73&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Leah McLay has apologised for the failures. (ABC News)

Ms McLay, who is acting in the role while Electoral Commissioner Mick Sherry is on leave, said the commission was taking steps "to prevent these issues happening again".

"I apologise to the voters and candidates for the inconvenience and confusion that was caused and to staff who experienced issues working for ECSA," she said.

The revelation of uncounted votes is one of a litany of problems arising from the March 21 state election and is the second such instance in the state, coming after the Electoral Commission revealed last week that it had [discovered more than 600 uncounted ballot papers](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-16/new-count-in-key-sa-state-election-seat-of-narungga/106570406) from a pre-poll booth in Port Pirie.

Asked last Thursday whether there could be other boxes of uncounted votes, Ms McLay said: "All ballots have been returned to us and we have not identified any other irregularities."

About 80 of the 600 voteswere from the closely contested seat of Narungga on the Yorke Peninsula, prompting a [count on Friday which confirmed victory for One Nation's Chantelle Thomas](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-17/narungga-new-count-ballots-results/106574886).

![One Nation's Chantelle Thomas at a media conference.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/dba6ca82fc6e52af24d2e67dfe2ad458?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=12&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

One Nation's Chantelle Thomas was confirmed as the Narungga MP. (ABC News)

The commission has also faced criticism over understaffing, delays at polling stations caused by electronic electoral roll log-in difficulties, and the handling of the First Nations Voice to Parliament vote.

ECSA previously apologised over pay delays, after a commission official wrote to staff to acknowledge that there were people who were "yet to be remunerated" more than a week after the election.

Attorney-General Kyam Maher described the discovery of more uncounted votes as "not good enough" and said he had suggested to the Electoral Commission that it "triple-check everything".

> "I've only received very initial advice from ECSA that there were a small number of votes that inadvertently weren't counted in the original count. ECSA will have to explain exactly what happened,"

he said.

"It doesn't affect the outcome of any result in the election, from what we know, but it is disappointing when these things happen. They shouldn't happen."

![A man wearing a suit and tie with a First Nations pattern on it looks off camera with a serious expression](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d290ac766f709e79dc421b7c53ebd8fe?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1667&cropW=2500&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Kyam Maher says the situation is "not good enough". (ABC News: Che Chorley)

He today reaffirmed the government's commitment to an independent review of the election — something the opposition also supported.

"In terms of a review of the election, we're keen to have that arm's length from ECSA," Mr Maher said.

He said Mick Sherry was "still the Electoral Commissioner" and that questions about ECSA's leadership were a matter for the commission.

## 'Unusual set of circumstances'

Opposition Deputy Leader Josh Teague described the discovery of uncounted ballots as "a most unusual set of circumstances".

"Additional votes are being found still. Clearly there are concerns about this process, the election process, from start to finish," he said.

"We want to know exactly how that's occurred and why, so that we can ensure that the high level of confidence that South Australians are entitled to have in their state election process can be upheld and maintained in the future."

![SA Liberal deputy leader MP Josh Teague on the steps of the state parliament building.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/0573459e008b6375ad4f98ce40782bdd?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Deputy Liberal Leader Josh Teague says there have been problems "from start to finish". (ABC News: Olivia Mason)

Mr Teague said it was a mistake to run the election for the First Nations Voice to Parliament at the same time as the state poll.

"I've heard all sorts of anecdotal feedback about how difficult that's been as a practical matter for those even who are eligible and might vote in both of those elections," he said.

"It's proving to be not such a great idea, but it compounds problems that we've seen now over these last few years in relation to a legislated state Voice."

Mr Maher said the turnout at the 2026 Voice election was "significantly higher" than at the first one, but [said problems arising from the recent Voice vote would be part of the review](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-07/call-to-review-sa-voice-to-parliament-voter-experience/106529974).

"I've had many people call me, call my office about their experience \[with\] the Voice elections and the difficulties they faced in voting — being sent out again and having to queue for a couple more hours to vote, being told they can't vote in the general election once they've voted in the Voice election — so it will necessarily have to look at how that was conducted," he said.

![A hand puts a lower house vote into a sealed ballot box as another person points to the top of the box](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/1160dee845aa613a7ec0afa50fa68503?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The commission said the "small number" of uncounted votes would not have affected the outcome, but its statement does not say how many were found. (ABC News: Trent Murphy)

The acting commissioner confirmed her backing for the independent review of the election.

"I will ensure that the expected independent external review receives everything it needs to ensure that no stone is left unturned and public confidence in the commission is upheld," Ms McLay said.

Emeritus Professor of politics at Adelaide University, Clem Macintyre, said it was a "worrying sign" that votes could be misplaced.

"There's normally a very close match between the numbers crossed off on the electoral roll and the number of ballot papers being admitted to the count," he said.

"I've not seen anything to suggest there's a lack of integrity in this process, but … there's clearly some inefficiencies that's allowed these lost votes to be misplaced and not found until well after the declaration of the results of the House of Assembly."

He said running elections were "very complex business" and there were a series of problems in the lead-up to and during polling day this year.

"The fact that there had been as many mistakes as they have undermines confidence that people might otherwise have in the electoral commission, so it's very important that all steps are taken to examine the causes of the problems this time round and ensure those don't occur again," he said.
